summary: Dark Star > Brokedown Palace shines; Bird Song and Playin' soar
blurb: The set delivers a towering Dark Star that morphs into a seamless Brokedown Palace, highlighted by soaring Bird Song and an expansive Playing In The Band jam. Jerry’s vocal delivery is strong and his guitar work shines throughout, while Keith provides soaring keyboards on the “Tiger” section.
review: September 16, 1972 at Boston Music Hall captures the Grateful Dead in top‑form during a classic early‑70s stretch. From the opening burst of Bertha through an inspired first set, the band builds to one of the night’s high points: a Dark Star that erupts into a fluid Brokedown Palace, backed by Keith Godchaux’s soaring piano and a rare Ned Lagin Wurlitzer texture. Bird Song soars with Garcia’s vocal clarity, and Playing In The Band stretches out into a marathon jam that feels both loose and purposeful. Phil Lesh drives the low end despite a faint bass imprint on some tapes, while Billy Kreutzmann keeps a tight, machine‑like pulse throughout. Bob Weir’s vocals slip a line or two on Big River but never lose the energy. Overall, the performance is vibrant, adventurous, and quintessentially 1972, making it a must‑listen for any Deadhead looking to experience that era’s electric chemistry.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Four of the five recordings are marked positive and praise the same highlights—Dark Star > Brokedown Palace, Bird Song, Playin', and strong Jerry guitar/vocals—while the lone mixed review offers no criticism. This consistent enthusiasm across multiple sources warrants a strong positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Decent partial soundboard capture with clear vocals and instrument detail; highlights Bird Song, Dark Star > Brokedown Palace, and up‑tempo Don't Ease Me In despite low bass.

key_highlights: Epic Dark Star → Brokedown Palace sequence with seamless transition
Soaring Bird Song showcases Garcia’s vocal strength
Extended Playing In The Band jam drives the set forward
Rare Ned Lagin/Wurlitzer guest spot on Dark Star adds a unique texture

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers one of his best‑sung shows; his guitar work shines especially on Dark Star, Bird Song and the up‑tempo Don't Ease Me In, with praised wah‑wah tones and elegant leads.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass drives the jams despite a faint recording presence; some listeners note moments where he sounds absent, but overall his foundation underpins the set.
band_performance.Bob: Weir contributes solid rhythm guitar and vocals, though a lyrical slip on Big River is noted; his playing remains steady throughout.
band_performance.Keith: Godchaux provides soaring keyboard work during the “Tiger” jam in Dark Star and adds a lush texture to the overall performance; guest Ned Lagin’s Wurlitzer on Dark Star > Brokedown Palace is highlighted as a rare early‑70s appearance.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann is described as a ‘machine chugging along,’ delivering tight, propulsive drumming that locks in with the band.
